Name: DEMOGRAPHICS
Notes: ONLY use as a domain header for organizing demographic information - NOT for coding individual patient demographics or characteristics.

Name: AGE RANGE
Notes: Age at which specific symptoms or impact occur

Name: 12 to 17 years of age
Notes: ONLY use when patient age is explicitly stated as 12-17 years: 'patient is 14', 'age 16', 'teenager', 'adolescent age' - NOT for grade level, developmental age, or inferred age without explicit statement.

Name: 18 to 29 years of age
Notes: ONLY use when patient age is explicitly stated as 18-29 years: 'patient is 22', 'age 25', 'young adult' - NOT for grade level, developmental age, or inferred age without explicit statement.

Name: 2 to 4 years of age
Notes: ONLY use when patient age is explicitly stated as 2-4 years: 'patient is 3', 'age 2', 'toddler age', 'preschool age' - NOT for grade level, developmental age, or inferred age without explicit statement.

Name: 30 years and older
Notes: ONLY use when patient age is explicitly stated as 30+ years: 'patient is 35', 'age 45', 'adult patient' - NOT for grade level, developmental age, or inferred age without explicit statement.

Name: 5 to 11 years of age
Notes: ONLY use when patient age is explicitly stated as 5-11 years: 'patient is 8', 'age 10', 'school age', 'elementary age' - NOT for grade level, developmental age, or inferred age without explicit statement.

Name: Under 2 years of age
Notes: ONLY use when patient age is explicitly stated as under 2 years: 'patient is 18 months', 'age 1', 'infant', 'baby under 2' - NOT for grade level, developmental age, or inferred age without explicit statement.

Name: Aggression
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver explicitly states patient physically attacks others: 'hits', 'bites', 'kicks', 'scratches', 'chokes', 'punches' - this aggression may be directed towards caregivers or non-caregivers — NOT for general defiance, tantrums, or pushing during play

Name: Abnormal play
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver describes very specific unusual play: obsessive lining up objects, repetitive scripting exact phrases during play, or playing with toys in highly unusual ways - NOT for simply preferring certain toys or age-inappropriate interests.

Name: Autism/Autistic Behavior
Notes: STOP! READ CAREFULLY: Use this code ONLY if the caregiver says the exact words 'autism' or 'autistic' or 'on the spectrum'. If you decide to classify this, address this note in your rationale and how it applies. NEVER EVER use this code for: 'diagnosed condition', 'this disease', '8p deletion syndrome', 'genetic condition', 'developmental delays', 'behavioral issues', 'she was diagnosed', 'he has a condition'. If you cannot find the exact words 'autism' or 'autistic' in the transcript, DO NOT USE THIS CODE. This is a common error - double check the transcript contains these exact words before coding.

Name: Consumption Behaviors, Abnormal (Includes changes in eating and/or drinking)
Notes: STOP! Use ONLY for actual eating/drinking abnormalities: overeating, food hoarding, excessive drinking, unusual food choices, eating too fast/slow. DO NOT use for: chewing on non-food items (books, toys, fingers), mouthing objects, oral sensory-seeking behaviors, or chewing for stimulation. These are sensory behaviors (code 29), NOT consumption behaviors. The patient must actually be CONSUMING/INGESTING something abnormally, not just chewing or mouthing for sensory input.

Name: Pica
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver explicitly states patient SWALLOWS/EATS non-food items: 'eats dirt', 'swallows paper', 'ingests hair', and this behavior has persisted over a period of at least one month — NOT for chewing, mouthing, or tasting non-food items without swallowing, or when the behavior has been present for less than one month

Name: Crying, abnormal (includes spontaneous, inconsolable, lack of)
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver describes extreme crying patterns: completely inconsolable for hours, crying for no apparent reason repeatedly, or complete absence of crying when pain/distress would be expected - NOT for normal fussiness or brief crying episodes.

Name: Disinhibition
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver describes patient acting without social boundaries: approaching strangers inappropriately, removing clothes in public, saying inappropriate things to strangers, disregarding social conventions, or showing poor risk assessment in social settings - behaviors stemming from a failure to resist temptations or urges in social contexts. — NOT for general impulsivity or rule-breaking that does not specifically involve violations of social boundaries or conventions.

Name: Social Behavior, abnormal (Includes inappropriate behavior, overly friendly, physical contact seeking/avoiding, attention seeking, interest in others, emotional immaturity)
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver describes very specific inappropriate social behaviors: touching strangers' faces, hugging unknown people, complete avoidance of all eye contact, or extreme social immaturity far beyond age expectations - NOT for shyness, preferring adults, or mild social delays.

Name: ADHD (includes hyperactivity, ADD)
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver explicitly states 'ADHD', 'ADD', 'attention deficit', or 'hyperactivity disorder' diagnosis OR describes medication for ADHD (Adderall, Ritalin, Concerta) - NOT for general hyperactivity, impulsivity, or attention problems without formal diagnosis.

Name: Impulsivity
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver describes extreme impulsive acts with potential danger: sudden bolting into traffic, grabbing knives, jumping from heights, or other clearly dangerous impulsive behaviors where there is an inability to inhibit or control behavior, acting without consideration of outcomes, difficulty establishing or following plans, and experiencing a sense of urgency that leads to uninhibited and uncontrolled actions — NOT for normal childhood impulsivity, interrupting, grabbing toys, or behaviors where the child can demonstrate some level of impulse control

Name: Excitable
Notes: Over'-'stimulation

Name: Lethargy (includes increasing with age)
Notes: REVIEW FEEDBACK: Only use for clearly abnormal lethargy/excessive sleepiness beyond normal tiredness. Reviewer noted uncertainty about what counts as abnormal. Requires clear indication this is pathological fatigue, not just being tired.

Name: Maladaptive behaviors (includes biting, hair'-'pulling, pinching slapping, yelling)
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver explicitly describes patient doing these specific behaviors to others: 'bites other kids', 'pulls people's hair', 'pinches others', 'slaps people', 'yells at others' - NOT for self-injury behaviors (use code 31) or aggressive behaviors covered by code 11.

Name: Mouth stuffing and pocket filling
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver describes extreme eating behaviors: stuffing mouth so full patient cannot chew/swallow safely, storing large amounts of food in cheeks like a chipmunk, or hoarding food in mouth - NOT for normal big bites or eating quickly.

Name: Oppositional behaviors, includes oppositional defiance disorder
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver explicitly states 'ODD', 'oppositional defiant disorder' diagnosis OR describes extreme defiance: refusing all requests, saying 'no' to everything consistently, deliberately doing opposite of instructions, with an enduring pattern of uncooperative, defiant, and hostile behavior towards authority figures that results in significant functional impairment — NOT for normal toddler defiance, occasional resistance, behaviors that involve major antisocial violations, or behaviors that are appropriate for the child's developmental stage

Name: Repetitive behaviors (includes OCD, hand-wringing, compulsions, rituals, loud repetitive vocalizations)
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver describes extreme repetitive behaviors that interfere with daily life: must perform rituals for hours, repetitive movements that prevent other activities, or compulsions that cause distress when interrupted - NOT for normal routines, preferences, or occasional repetitive play.

Name: Fixated interest, or atypical interest in or use of objects (such as water, crinkly items, mirrors, reflections, paper, twirling hair, music, outdoors, ceiling fans, vehicles, opening closing doors
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver describes obsessive, intense fascination with specific objects that dominates daily life: spending hours staring at fans, unable to function without touching mirrors, extreme distress when separated from specific object - NOT for normal strong interests or preferences.

Name: Perseveration (includes repetitive speech/verbalization)
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver describes speech stuck on same topic for extended periods: repeating same question 50+ times, unable to stop talking about one subject despite redirection, or echolalia that interferes with communication - NOT for normal repetition during learning or favorite topics.

Name: Sensory behavior, abnormal (sensory-seeking, spinning, eye'-'poking, hair brushing, touching hands)
Restlessness (includes agitation, exploratory mobility)
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver describes constant, persistent inability to stay still that interferes with daily activities: cannot sit for meals, constantly moving during quiet activities, or agitation that disrupts sleep/learning - NOT for normal active behavior, occasional fidgeting, or age-appropriate energy levels.

Name: Self'-'Injurious Behavior (include self'-'harm, head banging, trichotillomania, wound picking, skin picking, biting self)
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver explicitly describes patient causing physical harm to themselves: repeated head-banging causing bruises, skin picking causing wounds, biting self hard enough to leave marks - NOT for occasional scratching, mild hair twisting, or brief frustrated hitting that doesn't cause injury.

Name: Temper tantrums
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver describes extreme meltdowns far beyond typical tantrums: lasting over 30 minutes, requiring multiple adults to manage, or causing physical exhaustion; these abnormal temper tantrums occur more frequently, are more severe in symptoms, and are inappropriate for the child's developmental age — NOT for normal age-appropriate tantrums, brief crying episodes, typical frustration responses, or temper displays that occur as part of the normal developmental process

Name: Cardiorespiratory
Notes: ONLY use as a domain header for organizing cardiorespiratory conditions - NOT for coding individual patient symptoms or diagnoses.

Name: Cardiac Defect/Structural Abnormality (pulmonary atresia, murmur, mitral valve disease, enlarged, atrial septal defects, arrythmia, murmur, bradycardia, low heart rate)
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver or clinician explicitly states a diagnosed structural heart defect: 'ASD', 'hole in heart', 'valve disease', 'heart murmur', 'enlarged heart' with medical confirmation - NOT for heart rate variations, exercise intolerance, or vague heart concerns without structural diagnosis.

Name: Cardiac Failure (Heart Failure, Hypertrophy, Cardiomyopathy)
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver or clinician explicitly states 'heart failure', 'cardiomyopathy', 'enlarged heart muscle', or describes severe heart function problems requiring medication/intervention - NOT for heart murmurs, irregular rhythms, or structural defects without failure.

Name: Dystussia (difficulty coughing)
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver explicitly describes patient cannot cough effectively: 'can't clear throat', 'weak cough', 'unable to cough up mucus' causing respiratory problems - NOT for occasional coughing, throat clearing, or normal cough variations.

Name: Hypersecretion
Notes: excess fluid of all kinds

Name: Irregular Heart Rate
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver or clinician explicitly states 'irregular heartbeat', 'arrhythmia', 'heart skips beats', or describes documented rhythm abnormalities on EKG/monitor, including arrhythmias of sinus or ectopic origin, disturbances in impulse formation or conduction, and specific conditions like atrial fibrillation characterized by disorganized atrial activity without discrete P waves on EKG, with an undulating baseline or sharply circumscribed atrial deflections of varying amplitude and frequency ranging from 350-600 per minute — NOT for fast heart rate during activity, feeling heart beating, vague heart sensations, or any cardiac symptoms without explicit mention of rhythm irregularity or documentation on EKG/monitor

Name: Blood pressure, abnormal (hypertension, hypotension, high, low)
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver or clinician explicitly states 'high blood pressure', 'hypertension', 'low blood pressure', 'hypotension' with medical documentation or medication for BP - NOT for single elevated readings, white coat hypertension, or unconfirmed pressure concerns.

Name: Lower Respiratory Infections, recurrent, includes pneumonia
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver describes 2+ documented episodes of pneumonia or lower respiratory infections within a year: 'had pneumonia twice', 'keeps getting lung infections', multiple antibiotic courses for chest infections - NOT for single pneumonia, upper respiratory infections, or bronchitis.

Name: Respiration, Abnormal (Includes Asthma, Hypoxia, Restrictive Airway Disease)
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver explicitly states 'asthma', describes inhaler use, mentions oxygen needs, or clinician documents restrictive airway disease - NOT for occasional wheezing, exercise-induced breathing changes, or mild respiratory symptoms without diagnosis.

Name: Apneic Episodes
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver explicitly describes patient stops breathing: 'stops breathing while sleeping', 'holds breath for long periods', documented apneas on sleep study, recurrent episodes of apnea occurring during infancy, or recurrent episodes of apnea precipitated by factors such as illness, fatigue, or stress - where apnea involves a complete lack of breathing with no movement of respiratory muscles and no exchange of air in the lungs — NOT for breath-holding during crying, irregular breathing patterns, or snoring without apneas, single isolated apneic events, or instances where respiratory muscle movement or air exchange is still occurring

Name: Aspiration, Includes Silent
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver describes food/liquid going into lungs: 'chokes and food goes down wrong way', swallow study shows aspiration, clinician documents aspiration risk, or there is evidence of inspiration of foreign objects into the airway which may lead to aspiration pneumonia or when patients have a medical history of repeated episodes of aspiration pneumonia — NOT for occasional coughing while eating, gagging, or normal swallowing difficulties that do not result in material entering the upper respiratory tract

Name: Respiratory Distress/Failure
Notes: Only use for serious respiratory events: ventilator dependence, respiratory failure, acute distress requiring intervention. Brief neonatal apnea that self-resolved is NOT respiratory distress/failure.

Name: Structural Respiratory Abnormality (pulmonary atresia)
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver or clinician explicitly states diagnosed structural lung/airway abnormality: 'lung didn't develop right', 'airway malformation', 'pulmonary atresia' with medical confirmation - NOT for functional breathing problems or asthma without structural defects.

Name: Syncope (fainting)
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver explicitly describes patient faints or loses consciousness: 'passes out', 'blacks out', 'faints', falls unconscious with complete loss of awareness - NOT for dizziness, feeling faint, brief weakness, or nearly fainting without loss of consciousness.

Name: Tonsils/Adenoids, Abnormal
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver or clinician explicitly states 'enlarged tonsils', 'big adenoids', tonsillectomy/adenoidectomy performed, or sleep study shows obstruction from tonsils/adenoids - NOT for occasional sore throats, snoring without documented enlargement, or frequent colds.

Name: Upper Respiratory Infections, recurrent, sinusitus
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver describes 6+ colds/sinus infections per year or chronic sinusitis: 'always has a runny nose', 'constantly sick with colds', antibiotic treatment for recurrent sinus infections, or recurrent upper respiratory tract infections that may include otitis, pharyngitis, or tonsillitis, reflecting an increased susceptibility to respiratory infections in general — NOT for normal childhood colds, seasonal allergies, or occasional upper respiratory illness

Name: Cognition
Notes: ONLY use as a domain header for organizing cognitive conditions or threat-based cognitive biases that result in skewed perception, experience, or processing of internal or external stimuli — NOT for coding individual patient symptoms or diagnoses

Name: Abstract Concepts Difficult
Notes: (no concept of time for example, in the presence of normal intellectual ability) | Only use when caregiver explicitly describes difficulty with abstract thinking (time concepts, money, hypotheticals) IN THE PRESENCE OF otherwise normal intellectual ability. General cognitive delay is not this code.

Name: Cognitive Decline/Regression
Notes: Only use when caregiver describes clear DECLINE from a previous cognitive level (e.g., used to know letters, now does not). Fluctuating skills or slow progress is NOT cognitive decline.

Name: Memory Challenges
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver describes significant memory problems affecting daily life: 'forgets instructions immediately', 'can't remember what happened yesterday', requires constant reminders for routine tasks, or shows reduction in ability to retrieve information about how to perform activities such as riding a bike, driving a car, performing activities of daily living, or playing a musical instrument — NOT for occasional forgetfulness, distractibility, or age-appropriate memory limitations

Name: Reduced consciousness (includes coma, vegetative state, unconscious)
Notes: Abnormally diminished level of attention, responsiveness, or wakefulness.HP:0004372  Vegetative state: The absence of wakefulness and consciousness, but in contrast to a coma, there is involuntary opening of the eyes and movements such as teeth grinding, yawning, or thrashing of the extremities.

Name: Executive Function Skills, Impaired (logic, planning, time'-'management, organization)
Notes: Only use when caregiver explicitly describes problems with planning, organization, time management, or logical sequencing. Do NOT use for general cognitive slowness, processing delays, or lack of safety awareness.

Name: Global Developmental Delay (Children < 5, includes Vineland, VABS-3, lower adaptive behavior)
Notes:
https://hpo.jax.org/app/browse/term/HP:0001263
A delay in the achievement of motor or mental milestones in the domains of development of a child, including motor skills, speech and language, cognitive skills, and social and emotional skills. This term should only be used to describe children younger than five years of age.
Developmental retardation is any significant lag in development in the any or all of the physical, cognitive, behavioral, emotional, or social spheres. Note that the term intellectual disability refers to not merely a delay in development but rather a permanent limitation. Note that the term 'psychomotor retardation' is also used in some contexts to refer to a slowing of thought and physical movements as a result of major depression or intoxication.
Use when there are delays in two or more domains. Co-code with specific delays when applicable.

Name: Intellectual Disability (includes Cognitive Disability)
Notes:
Intellectual disability, previously referred to as mental retardation, is characterized by subnormal intellectual functioning that occurs during the developmental period. It is defined by an IQ score below 70.
This term should be used for children who are at least five years old. For younger children, consider using the term Global developmental delay (HP:0001263).

Name: Cognitive impairment
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver or clinician explicitly states "cognitive impairment" and more specific codes (ID/GDD) don't apply — describes general thinking, reasoning, or remembering difficulties without formal diagnosis. NOT for learning problems, attention issues, or specific skill delays. For children under 5, prefer [55] Global Developmental Delay unless cognitive impairment is explicitly stated. The highlighted text must demonstrate actual cognitive deficits, not just need for future help or support.

Name: Judgement Impaired: Danger awareness limited
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver describes patient has no understanding of safety dangers: walks into traffic without looking, approaches dangerous situations without fear, completely unaware of basic safety risks - NOT for typical childhood risk-taking, mild impulsivity, or needing supervision for complex safety situations.

Name: Memory Enhanced
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver explicitly describes patient has unusually exceptional memory abilities: 'remembers everything from years ago', 'can recite entire books from memory', photographic recall that amazes others - NOT for good memory for favorite topics, remembering routines well, or age-appropriate memory skills.

Name: Learning disability (includes dyslexia)
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver or clinician explicitly states 'learning disability', 'dyslexia', 'LD' diagnosis, or describes formal testing showing specific learning disorder - NOT for general academic struggles, developmental delays, or difficulty with school subjects without formal LD diagnosis.

Name: Communication
Notes: ONLY use as a domain header for organizing communication conditions, which encompasses any form of communication (verbal or non-verbal) that does not align with cultural expectations or developmental level — NOT for coding individual patient symptoms or diagnoses

Name: Dysarthria
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver or clinician explicitly describes slurred, effortful speech due to muscle weakness: 'speech is slurred', 'hard to understand because muscles don't work right', difficulty moving mouth/tongue for speech, or when they specifically describe neurological speech disorder characterized by poor articulation (which may be classified as spastic, flaccid, ataxic, hyperkinetic, hypokinetic, or mixed dysarthria depending on the involved neurological structures). — NOT for unclear speech due to language delays, articulation errors, or normal speech development variations.

Name: Echolalia
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver explicitly describes patient repeats words/phrases others say: 'copies everything we say', 'repeats TV shows word for word', immediate or delayed repetition of speech. The repetition may be automatic, imitative, and occur without explicit awareness. The repeated words or phrases are typically odd or used in a non-social manner. These can be words or phrases that the affected individual has heard or invented. — NOT for learning language by repetition, asking questions repeatedly, or normal language imitation during development.

Name: Speech Delay
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver explicitly states first words/sentences came much later than typical: 'didn't say first word until 3', 'no sentences until 4 years old', significantly behind speech milestones - NOT for mild delays, unclear speech, or children who are completely non-speaking (use code 72).

Name: Expressive Communication Decreased
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver describes patient has great difficulty expressing wants/needs: very limited vocabulary for age, extreme frustration trying to communicate, relies heavily on gestures because words are insufficient, and demonstrates a specific delay in acquiring language ability to communicate needs, wishes, or thoughts — NOT for mild speech delays, shyness, or preferring non-verbal communication when verbal is available

Name: Non-speaking communication through the use of eye contact, facial expression, body postures and gestures
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver describes patient deliberately uses non-verbal methods to communicate specific messages: points to request items, uses facial expressions to show emotions, intentional gestures to convey meaning. This includes assessment of abnormal facial expressions with specific focus on quantity, range, context-appropriateness, and quality during social interactions. — NOT for random gestures, stimming behaviors, or incidental non-verbal behaviors without communicative intent.

Name: Verbal Language can be used to Communicate (Includes few words)
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver describes patient can use some words/phrases meaningfully for communication: says a few words to request/protest, uses simple phrases to communicate needs, functional verbal communication even if limited - NOT for non-speaking patients, those who only echo without meaning, or vocalizations without communicative purpose.

Name: Sign language (includes ASL)
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver explicitly states patient uses formal sign language for daily communication: 'uses ASL', 'communicates with signs', 'we learned sign language' - NOT for simple gestures, pointing, waving, or informal hand signals without formal sign language training.

Name: Receptive Communication Decreased
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver describes patient has significant difficulty understanding spoken language: 'doesn't understand simple instructions', 'acts like deaf but hearing is normal', needs instructions repeated multiple times or simplified, or shows delayed development of receptive language skills — NOT for mild delays, selective listening, or age-appropriate comprehension limits

Name: Speech, Abnormal
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver describes distinctive speech quality abnormalities: 'sounds robotic', 'talks through nose', 'monotone voice', 'weird pitch', abnormal volume or rate (cadence) of speech that's consistently present - speech rhythm or pattern abnormalities may occur constantly throughout conversations or in periodic bursts lasting several minutes to hours — NOT for developmental articulation errors, volume issues not related to speech quality abnormalities, or speech delays covered by other codes

Name: Speech Impediment (includes pronunciation/verbal impairments '-' cerebellar)
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver describes specific articulation problems affecting understanding: 'can't say certain sounds', 'slurs words', 'speech is hard to understand because of pronunciation' - NOT for developmental speech delays, language delays, or speech clarity issues due to oral motor problems.

Name: Speech Regression
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver explicitly describes patient lost previously mastered words: 'used to say 20 words but stopped', 'talked in sentences then went silent', clear loss of established speech abilities - NOT for plateauing, temporary setbacks during illness, or slower progress.

Name: Strong Desire to Communicate
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver describes patient actively tries multiple ways to communicate despite limitations: 'tries so hard to tell us things', 'gets frustrated trying to communicate', 'keeps trying different ways to get message across' - NOT for basic interaction, following routines, or normal social engagement.

Name: Verbal Approximation, vocalizes but does not have full speech (baba, wawa)
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver describes patient makes syllable-like sounds but no clear words: 'says baba and mama but not meaningfully', 'makes sounds like words but unclear', consistent vocal attempts without real words - NOT for babbling in infants under 18 months or clear word attempts.

Name: Emotional
Notes: ONLY use as a domain header for organizing emotional conditions - NOT for coding individual patient symptoms or diagnoses.

Name: Anxiety (includes panic attacks, social anxiety, separation anxiety)
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver or clinician explicitly describes diagnosed anxiety or severe anxiety symptoms: 'has panic attacks', 'extreme separation anxiety requiring therapy', 'social anxiety prevents activities', anxiety medication prescribed - NOT for normal worries, shyness, or age-appropriate fears without clinical significance.

Name: Apathy
Notes: Apathy is a quantitative reduction of motivation and the initiation and persistence of goal'-'directed behavior, where the accompanying emotions, thoughts, and social interactions are also suppressed.

Name: Fear, Abnormal
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver describes extreme, irrational fears that significantly interfere with daily life: 'terrified of harmless objects', 'screams in panic at normal sounds', phobias requiring therapy, observable avoidance behaviors and abnormal responses to fear, including specific anxiety disorders characterized by avoidance of public places or crowds (agoraphobia) — NOT for age-appropriate fears, normal anxiety, reasonable caution, or typical cautious behaviors in genuinely threatening situations

Name: Frustration (symptom)
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver describes patient becomes extremely frustrated specifically due to communication barriers or physical limitations: 'gets so frustrated trying to tell us', 'cries because can't do things' - NOT for normal behavioral frustrations, tantrums, or general irritability.

Name: Happy Demeanor (includes Placid/Content)
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver consistently describes patient as unusually content and happy across all situations: 'always smiling', 'never gets upset about anything', 'content even when should be frustrated' - NOT for generally pleasant mood, good days, or normal happiness.

Name: Irritability
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver describes persistent, frequent irritability that interferes with daily functioning: 'irritated by everything', 'snaps at everyone constantly', requires behavior management, showing a proneness to anger with a tendency to become easily bothered or annoyed — NOT for occasional bad moods, age-appropriate grumpiness, or situational irritation

Name: Mood Swings (includes lack of self'-'regulation)
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver describes extreme, rapid mood changes within short periods: 'happy to screaming in seconds', 'emotions change constantly throughout day', unpredictable emotional shifts - NOT for normal emotional responses, age-appropriate mood variations, or gradual mood changes.

Name: Neuropsychiatric Issues (includes suspicion of bipolar, bipolar, hallucinations, catatonia, schizophrenia, depression)
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver or clinician explicitly states specific psychiatric diagnoses: 'bipolar disorder', 'sees things that aren't there', 'hears voices', 'catatonic episodes', psychiatric medication prescribed - NOT for general behavioral issues, mood problems, or developmental concerns without psychiatric diagnosis.

Name: Endocrine
Notes: ONLY use as a domain header for organizing endocrine conditions - NOT for coding individual patient symptoms or diagnoses.

Name: Adrenal Gland Abnormality (Includes adrenal insuffiency)
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver or clinician explicitly states diagnosed adrenal disorder: 'adrenal insufficiency', 'Addison's disease', 'adrenal glands don't work', steroid replacement therapy - NOT for fatigue, stress responses, or vague endocrine concerns without specific adrenal diagnosis.

Name: Diabetes type 2
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver or clinician explicitly states 'type 2 diabetes' diagnosis with blood sugar management: diabetes medication, insulin, blood sugar monitoring, insulin resistance, hyperinsulinemia, glucose intolerance, or hyperglycemia — NOT for pre-diabetes, gestational diabetes, type 1 diabetes, or family history of diabetes

Name: Growth hormone deficiency
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver or clinician explicitly states 'growth hormone deficiency' diagnosis with medical treatment: 'GH shots', 'growth hormone therapy', documented lab results showing concentration of growth hormone in blood circulation below normal limits, or confirmed by testing with growth hormone-releasing hormone administration — NOT for short stature, slow growth, or growth concerns without formal GH deficiency diagnosis

Name: Premenstrual syndrome (PMS) (includes PMDD)
Notes: premenstrual syndrome, premenstrual dysphoric disorder, catamenial epilepsy

Name: Puberty and gonadal disorders (includes PCOS)
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver or clinician explicitly states diagnosed disorders: 'PCOS', 'delayed puberty', 'precocious puberty', 'ovarian cysts', hormonal treatment for puberty issues - NOT for normal pubertal variations, early/late development within normal range, or general reproductive concerns.

Name: Thyroid, abnormal
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver or clinician explicitly states thyroid disorder diagnosis: 'hypothyroid', 'hyperthyroid', 'thyroid medication', 'abnormal thyroid labs', 'Synthroid' - NOT for fatigue, weight changes, or growth issues without documented thyroid abnormality.

Name: Gastrointestinal
Notes: ONLY use as a domain header for organizing gastrointestinal conditions - NOT for coding individual patient symptoms or diagnoses.

Name: Abdominal Symptoms
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver describes persistent abdominal pain or discomfort that doesn't fit other specific GI codes: 'stomach hurts frequently', 'belly pain most days', chronic abdominal discomfort - NOT for occasional stomach aches, meal-related discomfort, or symptoms better classified by specific GI codes.

Name: Appetite, Poor
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver describes persistently poor appetite affecting nutrition: 'never wants to eat', 'has to force food', 'appetite has been terrible for months', weight loss due to poor eating - NOT for picky eating, food preferences, or temporary appetite changes during illness.

Name: Constipation
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver describes chronic bowel problems: 'hasn't had a bowel movement in days', 'stools are very hard', 'strains to poop', uses Miralax/laxatives regularly, experiences difficult evacuation of feces, has fewer than 3 bowel movements per week lasting longer than three months, or reports sensations of anorectal obstruction or incomplete defecation — NOT for occasional hard stools, dietary-related changes, single episodes, or constipation lasting less than three months

Name: Diarrhea
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver describes frequent loose/watery stools (defined as three or more loose or watery bowel movements per day) as an ongoing problem: 'loose stools every day', 'chronic diarrhea' (persisting for over 4 weeks), 'watery bowel movements for weeks' — NOT for stomach bugs, temporary illness-related diarrhea, or occasional loose stools that don't meet the criteria of frequency (three or more daily) or chronicity (persisting over 4 weeks)

Name: Distended/protruding abdomen, bloating
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver describes persistent abdominal distension: 'belly is always swollen', 'stomach sticks out', 'looks pregnant from bloating', chronic abdominal distension - NOT for post-meal fullness, occasional bloating, or normal toddler belly appearance.

Name: Malrotation
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver or clinician explicitly states 'malrotation' diagnosis: 'intestinal malrotation', 'bowel malrotation', surgical correction for malrotation - a developmental anomaly involving abnormal rotation/fixation of the midgut during embryologic development, resulting in mislocalization of the small bowel, which can lead to volvulus (twisting of the intestine) potentially causing obstruction and necrosis — NOT for general GI issues, feeding problems, or abdominal symptoms without specific malrotation diagnosis; not to be used for other causes of intestinal obstruction or abdominal pain without confirmed malrotation

Name: Vomiting, Includes Cyclic
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver describes recurrent vomiting episodes: 'throws up every few weeks', 'cyclic vomiting', 'vomits for days then fine', pattern of repeated vomiting, or paroxysmal episodes of vomiting — NOT for single stomach bugs, occasional vomiting, or vomiting during illness

Name: Dysmotility (includes slow gastric emptying, neurologic gut, slow motility)
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver or clinician explicitly states motility disorder: 'slow gastric emptying', 'gastroparesis', 'food sits in stomach too long', documented motility study abnormalities - NOT for general feeding difficulties, reflux, or digestive complaints without motility diagnosis.

Name: Esophagitis
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver or clinician explicitly states 'esophagitis' diagnosis: 'inflamed esophagus', 'esophageal inflammation', endoscopy showing esophagitis, including specific types such as Candida esophagitis (an opportunistic infection common in immunosuppressed patients) or eosinophilic esophagitis (characterized by infiltration of numerous eosinophils, usually >15 per high power field, into the squamous epithelium of the esophagus) — NOT for heartburn, reflux symptoms, or swallowing difficulties without confirmed esophageal inflammation, and not for symptoms alone such as dysphagia, odynophagia, or retrosternal pain without explicit diagnosis of esophagitis or confirmatory endoscopic findings

Name: Gagging
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver describes frequent gagging during feeding: 'gags on most foods', 'chokes on liquids', 'gags when trying to eat', persistent feeding-related gagging - NOT for occasional gagging, texture preferences, or normal gag reflex responses.

Name: GI morphology, structure, abnormal
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver or clinician explicitly states structural GI abnormality: 'intestinal defect', 'bowel malformation', 'abnormal GI anatomy', structural abnormalities of the esophagus, structural anomalies of the gallbladder, or surgical repair of GI structure — NOT for functional GI problems, feeding issues, or symptoms without documented structural abnormalities

Name: Infant Feeding Issues (Includes poor sucking, chewing)
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver describes significant feeding problems in infancy: 'can't suck properly', 'weak suck', 'chokes on bottles', 'can't coordinate suck-swallow', feeding therapy needed - NOT for normal feeding variations, picky eating, or minor feeding adjustments.

Name: Leaky Gut (reduced nutrient absorption)
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver or clinician explicitly mentions 'leaky gut', 'intestinal permeability', 'malabsorption', or documented nutrient absorption problems with specific testing - NOT for general digestive issues, food sensitivities, or nutritional concerns without absorption diagnosis.

Name: Reflux (Includes GERD)
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver describes persistent reflux symptoms: 'spits up constantly', 'vomits acid', 'GERD diagnosis', uses reflux medications (Prilosec, Nexium, Pepcid, Prevacid, Zantac) - NOT for occasional spit-up, normal infant reflux, or single episodes.

Name: Gastrointestinal, other
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver describes GI symptoms that don't fit other specific codes: unusual digestive issues not captured elsewhere - NOT as a general category for any digestive complaint when more specific codes apply.

Name: Genitourinary
Notes: ONLY use as a domain header for organizing Genitourinary conditions, including tumors (abnormal growth of tissue) of the genitourinary system and anomalous structural findings of the fetal genitourinary system that can only be observed prenatally — NOT for coding individual patient symptoms or diagnoses, as this is strictly a classification header rather than a diagnostic term

Name: Genital system, abnormal (includes reproductive system, menses, irregular periods, cryptorchidism, torsion)
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver or clinician explicitly states abnormal genital system findings: 'irregular periods', 'cryptorchidism', 'torsion', documented abnormalities - NOT for normal variations, benign findings, or symptoms without specific abnormality.

Name: Genitourinary Infections (Includes pyelonephritis, chronic yeast infections, urinary tract infections (UTIs), recurrent UTIs)
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver describes recurrent GU infections (2+ episodes): 'keeps getting UTIs', 'chronic yeast infections', 'kidney infection', antibiotic treatment for repeated infections - NOT for single UTI episodes, occasional infections, or family history of infections.

Name: Incontinence (includes bed wetting)
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver describes persistent wetting issues beyond typical potty training: 'wets bed every night at age 7', 'has accidents during day', 'relies on pull-ups', ongoing incontinence affecting daily life - NOT for normal potty training process, occasional accidents, or nighttime wetting in children under 5.

Name: Renal abnormality (includes kidney stones)
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver or clinician explicitly states kidney problems: 'kidney stones', 'kidney defect', 'renal abnormality', 'kidney doesn't work right', nephrology referral - NOT for UTIs, bladder issues, or urinary symptoms without kidney involvement.

Name: Urinary System, Abnormal (includes bladder atonia, abnormal ureters, urine flow obstruction, structural defects)
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver or clinician explicitly states structural urinary abnormalities: 'bladder doesn't empty', 'blocked urine flow', 'abnormal ureters', 'bladder atonia', urological surgery needed - NOT for functional urinary issues, incontinence, or infections without structural defects.

Name: Growth
Notes: ONLY use as a domain header for organizing Growth conditions, which can include both prenatal and postnatal growth deficiency or slowing - NOT for coding individual patient symptoms or diagnoses. — NOT to be used for coding individual patient symptoms or diagnoses related to growth conditions.

Name: Failure to Thrive
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver or clinician explicitly states 'failure to thrive' or 'FTT' diagnosis: documented poor weight gain, growth faltering requiring medical intervention, or a child whose physical growth is substantially below the norm. — NOT for being small, slow growth within normal range, or feeding difficulties without formal FTT diagnosis.

Name: Obesity
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver or clinician explicitly states 'obesity' diagnosis or BMI in obese range: 'doctor says obese', 'BMI shows obesity', weight management program, including specific classifications such as Class 2 obesity (BMI 35-39.9 kg/m²) or BMI of 40 kg/m² or higher — NOT for being overweight, large for age, or weight concerns without formal obesity diagnosis

Name: Short stature
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver or clinician explicitly documents height below expected percentiles: 'short stature', 'height under 3rd percentile', endocrinology referral for short stature, height more than 2 standard deviations below the mean for age and gender, disproportionate short stature characterized by short limbs with average-sized trunk, or moderate short stature defined as height more than -3 SD but not more than -4 SD from the mean corrected for age and sex. — NOT for being small for age, family history of short stature, or subjective concerns about height without documented percentiles.

Name: Tall stature, long limbs
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver or clinician explicitly documents unusually tall height or long limbs: 'abnormally tall for age', 'height above 97th percentile', 'arms and legs are unusually long', endocrinology referral for tall stature, increased arm span to height ratio (>1.05), reduced upper-to-lower segment ratio (<0.85), or unusually slim extremities, hands, and feet — NOT for normal tall growth, family history of tallness, or subjective observations without documented measurements

Name: Body weight, abnormal (includes overweight, underweight, obesity, weight gain)
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver or clinician describes significant weight abnormalities not meeting specific obesity/underweight criteria: 'gained 20 pounds quickly', 'lost too much weight', 'weight fluctuates dramatically', concerning weight changes requiring monitoring - NOT for normal weight variations, growth spurts, or weight issues better classified by obesity/underweight codes.

Name: Underweight
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver or clinician explicitly states patient is clinically underweight: 'doctor says underweight', 'BMI too low', 'needs to gain weight', nutritional intervention for underweight - NOT for being thin, small appetite, or normal lean build without clinical underweight diagnosis.

Name: Hearing
Notes: ONLY use as a domain header for organizing Hearing conditions and decreased magnitude of sensory perception of sound - NOT for coding individual patient symptoms or diagnoses. — NOT for coding individual patient symptoms or diagnoses.

Name: Deafness
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver or clinician explicitly states patient is deaf or has profound hearing loss: 'completely deaf', 'can't hear anything', 'profoundly deaf', uses cochlear implants, deaf school enrollment, or has congenital deafness (present at birth) or infantile-onset deafness occurring before speech acquisition — NOT for hearing impairment, partial hearing loss, or needing hearing aids without complete deafness

Name: Hearing impariment
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver or clinician explicitly documents partial hearing loss: 'hearing loss', 'hard of hearing', 'hearing aids', failed hearing tests, audiology referral for hearing loss, including cases of mixed type hearing loss which combines conductive and sensorineural impairment — NOT for temporary hearing issues, ear infections affecting hearing, or normal hearing variations without documented impairment

Name: Hearing test, abnormal
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver or clinician explicitly states hearing test results were abnormal: 'failed hearing test', 'abnormal audiogram', 'hearing test showed problems', audiology follow-up recommended, decreased magnitude of sensory perception of sound documented in testing, or specifically documented mixed hearing loss (combination of conductive and sensorineural hearing impairment) — NOT for missed hearing screens, behavioral hearing concerns, suspected hearing issues without documented abnormal test results, or subjective reports of hearing difficulties without formal testing confirmation

Name: Otitis/Ear Infections
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver describes recurrent ear infections (2+ episodes per year): 'keeps getting ear infections', 'chronic ear infections', 'ear infections every few months', frequent antibiotic treatment for ears - NOT for single ear infection, occasional infections, or ear pain without documented infection.

Name: Motor
Notes: ONLY use as a domain header for organizing Motor conditions including motor neuron wasting, developmental delay in acquiring motor skills, and movement-based tics affecting discrete muscle groups - NOT for coding individual patient symptoms or diagnoses. — NOT for coding individual patient symptoms or diagnoses or for specific clinical documentation of individual motor-related manifestations.

Name: Apraxia (including of speech)
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver or clinician explicitly states 'apraxia' or 'apraxia of speech' diagnosis: 'has apraxia', 'motor planning problems', 'apraxia of speech', speech therapy for apraxia - NOT for general speech delays, articulation problems, or motor difficulties without specific apraxia diagnosis.

Name: Fatigue
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver describes persistent, abnormal tiredness affecting daily activities for six months or longer: 'always exhausted', 'no energy for normal activities', 'sleeps all the time but still tired', fatigue interfering with function, lack of motivation — NOT for normal tiredness, age-appropriate rest needs, or temporary fatigue during illness

Name: Fine motor skills, Abnormal
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver describes significant difficulties with hand/finger skills: 'can't hold pencil properly', 'can't button clothes', 'drops things constantly', 'can't use utensils', occupational therapy for fine motor, or other issues affecting wrists, hands, fingers, feet, and toes that represent a specific motor delay in finger and hand control abilities development — NOT for age-appropriate fine motor development, mild clumsiness, normal learning curves, or developmental challenges that fall within expected ranges

Name: Gross motor skills, Abnormal
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver describes significant difficulties with large motor skills such as: 'can't walk up stairs', 'falls constantly', 'can't run like other kids', 'very unsteady walking', has difficulty with control of large muscles needed for sitting and crawling, requires physical therapy for gross motor issues involving movement of large muscles in arms, legs, and other large body parts — NOT for age-appropriate motor development, minor coordination issues, or normal variation in motor skills

Name: Motor delay
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver describes significant delays in motor milestones: 'didn't walk until 2 years', 'couldn't sit up until 10 months', 'never crawled', documented motor milestone delays requiring intervention - NOT for minor variations, being cautious, or milestones within normal range variations.

Name: Movement Disorder
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver or clinician explicitly describes abnormal movement patterns: 'involuntary movements', 'chorea', 'dystonia', 'movement disorder', neurology referral for movement problems, or documented changes in coordination and speed of voluntary movements that indicate a neurological movement abnormality — NOT for normal fidgeting, restlessness, or voluntary movements that appear unusual but don't have documented neurological significance

Name: Ataxia
Notes: Cerebellar ataxia refers to ataxia due to dysfunction of the cerebellum. This causes a variety of elementary neurological deficits including asynergy (lack of coordination between muscles, limbs and joints), dysmetria (lack of ability to judge distances that can lead to under'-' or overshoot in grasping movements), and dysdiadochokinesia (inability to perform rapid movements requiring antagonizing muscle groups to be switched on and off repeatedly).  HP:0001251 HP:0001251

Name: Choreoathetosis (includes chorea and athetosis)
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver or clinician explicitly describes specific involuntary movement patterns: 'writhing movements', 'chorea', 'athetosis', 'choreoathetosis', neurological diagnosis of movement disorder - NOT for general restlessness, fidgeting, or voluntary movements that appear unusual.

Name: Dyskinesia
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver or clinician explicitly describes involuntary, uncontrolled movements: 'dyskinesia', 'involuntary movements', 'movements she can't control', medication-induced movement problems, or diminished voluntary movements; these may manifest as dystonic, choreic, or ballistic movements, or a combination thereof, occurring in bouts without loss of consciousness — NOT for voluntary movements, fidgeting, restlessness, normal movement variations, or involuntary movements associated with loss of consciousness

Name: Dyspraxia, Motor (impaired coordination of movement)
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver or clinician explicitly states 'dyspraxia' or describes severe motor planning difficulties: 'has dyspraxia', 'can't plan movements', 'motor planning problems', therapy for dyspraxia - NOT for general clumsiness, learning new skills, or coordination difficulties without dyspraxia diagnosis.

Name: Gait Abnormality
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver describes distinctly abnormal walking patterns: 'walks on toes constantly', 'walks on knees', 'very wide-based walking', 'limps', 'abnormal gait', physical therapy for gait, disruption of ability to walk, or episodes of freezing/marked reduction in forward progression of feet despite intention to walk — NOT for temporary walking variations, learning to walk, or minor gait preferences without functional impact

Name: Balance, unsteady
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver describes significant balance problems affecting daily activities: 'falls constantly', 'can't stand without support', 'very unsteady on feet', 'balance is terrible', physical therapy for balance, or clinical evaluation showing abnormal retropulsion test results (taking three or more steps backward, true retropulsion where patient continues stepping backward without regaining balance, or falls backward without taking corrective steps). — NOT for occasional falls, learning to walk, age-appropriate balance development, or when retropulsion test shows normal response (correction with one or two large steps or hip hinging with forward arm swing).

Name: Non Ambulatory
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver explicitly states patient cannot walk independently: 'doesn't walk', 'non-ambulatory', 'wheelchair bound', 'can't walk at all', uses wheelchair as primary mobility - NOT for children learning to walk, temporary mobility issues, or walking with assistance occasionally.

Name: Tremor/Tremulous Movements of Limbs
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver describes obvious tremor or shaking movements: 'hands shake', 'tremor in arms', 'shaky movements', 'tremulous', neurological evaluation for tremor - NOT for normal movement variations, excitement-related shaking, or temporary trembling.

Name: Oral Motor Impairment (Includes tongue thrusting, tongue abnormalities, macroglossia, sucking and swallowing disorders, frequent drooling, excessive chewing, mouthing behaviors, and feeding difficulties, teeth grinding)
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver describes significant oral motor problems: 'can't swallow properly', 'tongue is too big', 'drools constantly', 'can't chew food', 'chews everything', speech/feeding therapy for oral motor - NOT for normal drooling in toddlers, occasional choking, or mild feeding preferences.

Name: Paralysis (includes hemiplegia, limb, arm, leg paralysis, no voluntary movement)
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver or clinician explicitly states complete loss of movement: 'paralyzed', 'no movement in arm', 'hemiplegia', 'can't move leg at all', 'completely paralyzed' - NOT for weakness, reduced movement, temporary immobility, or partial movement limitations.

Name: Progressive loss of skills (regression) (includes fine and/or gross motor skills)
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver explicitly describes patient lost previously acquired motor abilities: 'used to walk but stopped', 'could use hands but can't anymore', 'lost motor skills over time', clear regression in motor function - NOT for plateauing development, temporary setbacks, or slower-than-expected progress.

Name: Musculoskeletal
Notes: ONLY use as a domain header for organizing Musculoskeletal conditions (system consisting of bones, muscles, cartilage, tendons, ligaments, joints, and connective tissue that supports body weight, maintains body position, and produces body movements) — NOT for coding individual patient symptoms or diagnoses related to the musculoskeletal system

Name: Craniofacial abnormalities (Includes: skull abnormalities, torticollis, nose abnormalities, midline retraction, abnormal eyebrows, puffy cheeks, long chin, cleft palate, high arched palate, prominent mandible, jaw abn...
Dental Abnormalities (Includes abnormal dentition, large front teeth, missing adult teeth)
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver or clinician explicitly documents tooth abnormalities: 'missing teeth', 'extra large front teeth', 'abnormal tooth formation', 'dental defects', orthodontic treatment for abnormalities - NOT for normal tooth development, delayed eruption within normal range, or cosmetic concerns without structural abnormalities.

Name: Hands and Feet Abnormalities (includes hypoplastic distal phlanges in the hand and feet, broad hallux phalanx and thumb, proximal placement of thumb, arachnotidactyly fingers, curled fingers, Navicular bone drop,  Cal...
Hernias
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver or clinician explicitly states hernia diagnosis: 'inguinal hernia', 'umbilical hernia' (which involves protrusion of abdominal contents through a defect in the abdominal wall musculature around the umbilicus, with skin and subcutaneous tissue overlying the defect), 'hernia', or when surgical repair of hernia is scheduled/completed — NOT for normal umbilical prominence in infants, abdominal muscle separation, or vague bulging without hernia diagnosis

Name: Joint Abnormalities (Includes laxity, hypermobility, pain, radial head dislocation, congenital hip dislocation)
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver or clinician explicitly documents joint problems: 'hip dislocation', 'joints are too loose', 'hypermobile joints', 'joint pain', orthopedic treatment for joint issues - NOT for normal flexibility, occasional joint pain, or minor joint variations without documented abnormalities.

Name: Poor head control, includes lag
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver describes significant head control problems beyond expected age: 'can't hold head up at 6 months', 'head flops back', 'poor head control', 'head lag', physical therapy for head control, or when head lag persists beyond 3 to 4 months of age when it should normally be absent. Head lag is observed when the head seems to flop around or lags posteriorly behind the trunk. — NOT for normal newborn head lag, age-appropriate head control development, or mild delays within normal range.

Name: Rib and Chest Abnormalities (Includes sternum malformation, wide ribs)
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver or clinician explicitly documents chest/rib structural abnormalities: 'chest malformation', 'rib defects', 'sternum abnormality', 'pectus', surgical repair recommended - NOT for normal chest variations, mild asymmetry, or respiratory issues without structural chest abnormalities.

Name: Skeletal Abnormalities
Notes: REVIEW FEEDBACK: Reviewer feedback: I'm not sure if these are considered "abnormalities"

Name: Fractures, unexplained, fragility
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver describes multiple unexplained fractures or fragility fractures: 'bones break easily', 'fractured from minor fall', 'multiple unexplained fractures', 'fragile bones', evaluation for bone disease, or when there is evidence of abnormal reduction in bone strength which is the underlying cause of increased fracture tendency — NOT for single traumatic fractures, normal childhood injuries, or fractures with clear cause

Name: Hip/Pelvis Abnormalities (Includes bilateral hip dysplasia)
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver or clinician explicitly documents hip/pelvis structural problems: 'hip dysplasia', 'hip dislocation', 'pelvic abnormality', orthopedic treatment for hip issues, harness/surgery for hips - NOT for normal hip development, temporary hip positioning, or minor hip variations without documented dysplasia.

Name: Long Bone Abnormalities (Includes twisted, shortened tibia and/or fibula)
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver or clinician explicitly documents long bone structural defects: 'shortened leg bones', 'twisted tibia', 'bone malformation', 'leg length discrepancy', orthopedic surgery for bone defects - NOT for normal bone growth variations, temporary growth differences, or functional issues without structural bone abnormalities.

Name: Skeletal Maturation Abnormalities (Includes bone age advanced, bone age delayed, osteoporosis, osteopenia, soft bones)
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver or clinician explicitly documents bone maturation problems: 'bone age delayed', 'osteoporosis', 'soft bones', 'bone density problems', endocrinology referral for bone issues - NOT for normal growth variations, size differences, or general bone health concerns without documented maturation abnormalities.

Name: Spine Abnormalities (Includes scoliosis, spina bifida, kyphosis, spinal stenosis, saccral dimple)
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver or clinician explicitly documents spine structural abnormalities: 'scoliosis', 'spina bifida', 'curved spine', 'sacral dimple', 'spine malformation', orthopedic/neurosurgical treatment for spine - NOT for poor posture, back pain, or minor spinal variations without documented structural abnormalities.

Name: Spasms and Contractures (Includes contractions, involuntary movement, muscle cramps)
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver explicitly describes involuntary muscle spasms or fixed contractures: 'muscles spasm uncontrollably', 'muscle cramps frequently', 'joints are contracted and won't straighten', physical therapy for contractures - NOT for normal muscle tightness, temporary cramps, or voluntary muscle tension.

Name: Ankle pronation (some inward rolling of ankle and foot)
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver or clinician explicitly documents excessive ankle/foot pronation: 'feet roll inward severely', 'ankles collapse inward', 'overpronation', orthotic treatment for pronation - NOT for normal foot positioning, mild inward rolling, or occasional foot posture variations.

Name: Stiffness (Includes muscle stiffness, joint stiffness)
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver describes persistent muscle or joint stiffness affecting function: 'muscles are always stiff', 'joints won't bend properly', 'body is rigid', physical therapy for stiffness - NOT for normal muscle tension, temporary stiffness after activity, or age-appropriate muscle tone.

Name: Neurological
Notes: ONLY use as a domain header for organizing Neurological conditions - NOT for coding individual patient symptoms or diagnoses.

Name: Cataplexy (Includes with narcolepsy, catatonia)
Notes:
HP:0002524
Cataplexy is a frequent feature of narcolepsy, a neurologic disorder characterized by excessive daytime sleepiness and manifestations of disrupted rapid eye movement sleep stage.
A sudden and transient episode of bilateral loss of muscle tone, often triggered by emotions.

Name: Neuropathy (includes peripheral)
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver or clinician explicitly states 'neuropathy' or describes nerve damage symptoms: 'tingling in hands/feet', 'numbness', 'burning sensation', 'peripheral neuropathy', neurological testing showing nerve damage - NOT for occasional tingling, normal sensory variations, or temporary numbness without neuropathy diagnosis.

Name: Cerebral Palsy
Notes: Cerebral palsy describes a group of permanent disorders of the development of movement and posture, causing activity limitation, that are attributed to nonprogressive disturbances that occurred in the developing fetal or infant brain. The motor disorders of cerebral palsy are often accompanied by disturbances of sensation, perception, cognition, communication, and behaviour, by epilepsy, and by secondary musculoskeletal problems. HP:0100021, SNOMEDCT_US:128188000

Name: Pain sensation, abnormal
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver describes significantly altered pain response: 'doesn't feel pain when injured', 'overreacts to minor pain', 'pain sensitivity is abnormal', 'can't tell when hurt', reduced ability to perceive painful stimuli, or pain abnormalities that may result from abnormalities in the specific nerve fibers that carry pain impulses to the brain — NOT for normal pain responses, age-appropriate pain reactions, or temporary pain sensitivity changes

Name: Cry, Distinctive
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver describes a characteristic, unusual cry pattern that stands out: 'cry sounds different from other babies', 'has a very distinctive cry', 'cry is high-pitched and unusual', 'strange sounding cry', 'cry sounds similar to the meowing of a kitten', or cry is characterized by unusually shortened and detached vocalizations — NOT for normal crying variations, loud crying, or frequent crying without distinctive quality

Name: EEG Abnormal
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver or clinician explicitly states EEG test results were abnormal: 'EEG showed abnormalities', 'brain waves were abnormal', 'abnormal electroencephalogram', 'interictal EEG abnormalities', 'periodically recurring abnormalities in the EEG', or neurology follow-up for abnormal EEG. Note that EEG records the brain's spontaneous electrical activity from multiple electrodes placed on the scalp, and abnormalities may refer to unusual brain wave patterns detected between seizures. — NOT for suspected seizures, behavioral concerns, EEG ordered without confirmed abnormal results, or cases where multiple EEG recordings were still needed to detect abnormalities but no abnormalities were yet confirmed.

Name: Head Circumference, abnormal
Notes: Only use when abnormal head circumference is explicitly stated (microcephaly or macrocephaly). Rapid head growth that was a concern but not confirmed abnormal does NOT qualify — use [170] Macrocephaly if confirmed large.

Name: Macrocephaly, large head circumference
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver or clinician explicitly documents large head circumference above normal percentiles: 'macrocephaly', 'head circumference above 97th percentile', 'large head', 'big head', neurology evaluation for large head, or occipitofrontal circumference greater than 97th centile compared to appropriate age-matched and sex-matched normal standards. This can be present with onset at birth. — NOT for family history of large heads, subjective observations, or normal large head variations without documented measurements.

Name: Microcephaly, small head circumference, includes primary and secondary
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver or clinician explicitly documents small head circumference below normal percentiles, such as 'microcephaly', 'head circumference below 3rd percentile', 'head circumference below 2 standard deviations below the mean for age and gender', 'small head', 'tiny head', or genetics/neurology referral for small head. Includes microcephaly resulting from insufficient head growth after birth. — NOT for family history of small heads, subjective observations, or normal small head variations without documented measurements.

Name: Hydrocephalus
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver or clinician explicitly states 'hydrocephalus' diagnosis: 'fluid on the brain', 'hydrocephalus', 'enlarged ventricles', shunt placement, neurosurgery consultation for hydrocephalus - includes cases of active distension of the ventricular system due to inadequate CSF passage, normal pressure hydrocephalus (characterized by enlarged cerebral ventricles with normal CSF pressure upon lumbar puncture), and obstructive hydrocephalus (characterized by obstruction of CSF flow within the cerebral ventricular system or in the outlets to the arachnoid space) — NOT for large head alone, suspected fluid, or brain imaging ordered without confirmed hydrocephalus diagnosis - do not include cases where there is no explicit hydrocephalus diagnosis or cases where the ventricular enlargement has not been formally diagnosed as hydrocephalus by a medical professional

Name: Hemiplegic, migraine
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver or clinician explicitly states 'hemiplegic migraine' diagnosis: 'hemiplegic migraines', 'migraines with paralysis', 'weakness during migraines', neurology treatment for hemiplegic migraines - NOT for regular migraines, headaches with nausea, or weakness unrelated to migraine episodes.

Name: MRI Abnormal
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver or clinician explicitly states MRI results were abnormal such as: 'MRI showed abnormalities', 'brain MRI was abnormal', 'abnormal MRI findings', neurology follow-up for abnormal MRI, signal abnormalities on MRI of the brainstem region of the brain, or deviation from normal signal on magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the thalamus region of the brain — NOT for MRI ordered, brain imaging planned, or normal MRI results without confirmed abnormalities

Name: Agenesis of the Corpus Callosum
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver or clinician explicitly states 'agenesis of corpus callosum', 'ACC' diagnosis, 'corpus callosum didn't develop', 'missing connection between brain halves', 'ACC', neurology/genetics referral for corpus callosum agenesis, partial agenesis of corpus callosum, or underdevelopment of the corpus callosum resulting from failure in cellular proliferation and migration, axonal growth, or glial patterning at the midline — NOT for other brain abnormalities, developmental delays, or brain imaging without specific ACC diagnosis

Name: Chiari Malformation
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver or clinician explicitly states 'Chiari malformation' diagnosis: 'Chiari I/II malformation', 'brain extends into spinal canal', 'Chiari', neurosurgery consultation for Chiari; includes documented downward displacement of cerebellar tonsils and/or medulla through the foramen magnum, cerebellar tonsils projecting 5 mm below the foramen magnum (McRae Line), herniation of hindbrain (cerebellum with or without brainstem) into a low occipital or high cervical meningoencephalocele, or hydrocephalus resulting from CSF obstruction specifically attributed to Chiari malformation — NOT for other brain abnormalities, headaches, or neck problems without specific Chiari diagnosis; not for cases where cerebellar tonsils are rounded or project less than 5 mm below the foramen magnum; not for hindbrain herniation due to other causes not explicitly identified as Chiari malformation

Name: Corpus Callosum Defects
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver or clinician explicitly states corpus callosum abnormalities other than complete agenesis, including: 'partial corpus callosum', 'thin corpus callosum', 'corpus callosum dysgenesis', 'callosal abnormality', increased vertical dimension of the corpus callosum (visualized by sagittal sections on MRI), or specific morphological abnormalities such as hump-shaped, kinked, or striped corpus callosum that lacks an anatomically distinct genu and splenium — NOT for complete agenesis of corpus callosum (use code 176), other brain abnormalities, developmental delays without specific callosal defects, or non-specific neurological findings without explicit mention of corpus callosum abnormalities

Name: Cortical defects
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver or clinician explicitly states cortical malformations or defects: 'cortical dysplasia', 'brain cortex malformation', 'cortical abnormalities', 'polymicrogyria', 'lissencephaly' - NOT for other brain abnormalities, developmental delays, or brain imaging without specific cortical malformation diagnosis.

Name: Delayed myelination
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver or clinician explicitly states MRI shows delayed myelination: 'delayed myelination on MRI', 'white matter not developing normally', 'myelin delay', neurology follow-up for myelination delay - NOT for general developmental delays, other brain abnormalities, or white matter changes without specific myelination delay diagnosis.

Name: Progressive brain atrophy/ neurodegeneration
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver or clinician explicitly states progressive brain volume loss or neurodegeneration: 'brain is shrinking', 'progressive brain atrophy', 'neurodegeneration', 'brain tissue loss over time', serial MRIs showing worsening - NOT for static brain abnormalities, developmental delays, or single brain imaging without progressive changes.

Name: Ventricle Anomalies (Other)
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver or clinician explicitly states ventricular abnormalities not covered by hydrocephalus: 'enlarged ventricles', 'abnormal ventricle shape', 'ventricular abnormalities', 'something wrong with ventricles' - NOT for hydrocephalus (use code 172), normal ventricular variations, or other brain abnormalities without specific ventricular involvement.

Name: Proprioception abnormal
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver describes poor body position awareness affecting function: 'doesn't know where body is in space', 'bumps into things constantly', 'can't tell arm position without looking', 'poor body awareness', occupational therapy for proprioception, loss or impairment of the sensation of joint position, particularly affecting distal joints, or impairment in the sensation of the relative position of body parts — NOT for occasional clumsiness, normal exploration, or mild coordination issues without body awareness problems

Name: Reflexes, abnormal (hyporeflexia, hyperreflexia)
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver or clinician explicitly documents abnormal reflexes: 'reflexes too strong', 'no reflexes', 'hyperreflexia', 'hyporeflexia', neurological exam showing reflex abnormalities - NOT for normal reflex variations, startle responses, or reflexes not formally tested.

Name: Seizures (focal, tonic'-'clonic, myoclonic, resistant or progressive seizure type)
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver or clinician explicitly describes seizure episodes: 'had a seizure', 'convulsions', 'tonic-clonic seizure', 'focal seizures', 'myoclonic jerks', anti-seizure medication prescribed - NOT for staring spells without confirmed seizures, brief episodes, or suspected seizures without clinical confirmation.

Name: Absence Seizures
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver or clinician explicitly describes or diagnoses absence seizures: 'absence seizures', 'staring spells with EEG confirmation', 'petit mal seizures', 'spaces out and unresponsive', absence seizure medication; may typically start before age 4; may present with eyelid myoclonia (forced upward jerking of eyelids during seizure); classified as generalized non-motor/dialeptic seizures of electrographically generalized onset where motor phenomena are minor compared to non-motor features — NOT for attention problems, daydreaming, or staring without EEG-confirmed absence seizures

Name: Febrile Seizures
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver explicitly describes seizures occurring with fever: 'seizure when fever was high', 'febrile seizure', 'convulsions during fever', 'seizure with temperature'. Febrile seizures typically occur with fever of at least 38 degrees Celsius, in the absence of central nervous system infection, severe metabolic disturbance or other alternative precipitant, and typically affect children between the ages of 3 months and 6 years. These may be simple (generalized, short duration <15 minutes, not recurring within 24 hours) or complex (focal semiology, prolonged >15 minutes, or multiple seizures within 24 hours). — NOT for fever alone, behavioral changes with fever, seizures unrelated to fever episodes, or seizures caused by acute nervous system disease, intracranial infection, or severe metabolic disturbance.

Name: Focal (Partial) Seizures
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver or clinician explicitly describes or diagnoses focal seizures: 'focal seizures', 'partial seizures', 'seizures start in one part of brain', 'focal epilepsy', seizures affecting specific body parts - NOT for generalized seizures, staring spells, or other seizure types without focal characteristics.

Name: Aware (Simple)
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver or clinician explicitly describes focal seizures where patient remains aware: 'simple focal seizures', 'aware during seizures', 'conscious during episodes', 'doesn't lose awareness' - NOT for other seizure types, episodes with impaired awareness, or non-seizure events.

Name: Unaware (Complex)
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver or clinician explicitly describes focal seizures with impaired awareness: 'complex focal seizures', 'loses awareness during seizures', 'unresponsive during episodes', 'doesn't remember seizures' - NOT for absence seizures, episodes with maintained awareness, or non-seizure events.

Name: Infantile Spasms (Includes West Syndrome)
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver or clinician explicitly describes or diagnoses infantile spasms: 'infantile spasms', 'West syndrome', 'baby has spasms', 'jerking movements in clusters', ACTH treatment for spasms - NOT for normal startle reflexes, other seizure types, or muscle spasms without infantile spasm diagnosis.

Name: Intractable Seizures, Refractory
Notes: When seizures can't be completely controlled by medicines. (Intractable means "not easily managed or relieved

Name: Nocturnal Epilepsy (seizures in sleep, night)
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver explicitly describes seizures occurring during sleep: 'seizures only at night', 'seizures during sleep', 'nocturnal epilepsy', 'convulsions while sleeping' - NOT for nighttime behaviors, sleep disturbances, or daytime seizures without specific nocturnal pattern.

Name: Postictal Abnormality (Includes regression, extended fatigue)
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver describes persistent problems after seizures: 'very tired for days after seizures', 'loses skills after seizures', 'takes long time to recover from seizures', 'postictal confusion lasting hours' - NOT for normal post-seizure tiredness, brief recovery periods, or fatigue unrelated to seizures.

Name: Rolandic Seizures (during transition between sleeping and waking)
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver or clinician explicitly describes seizures occurring during sleep-wake transitions: 'seizures when falling asleep', 'seizures when waking up', 'rolandic epilepsy', 'benign rolandic seizures' - NOT for other seizure types, sleep disturbances, or seizures at other times without specific sleep-wake transition pattern.

Name: Status Epilepticus
Notes:
Status epilepticus is a type of prolonged seizure resulting either from the failure of the mechanisms responsible for seizure termination or from the initiation of mechanisms which lead to abnormally prolonged seizures (after time point t1). It is a condition that can have long'-'term consequences (after time point t2), including neuronal death, neuronal injury, and alteration of neuronal networks, depending on the type and duration of seizures.
HP:0002133

Name: Tethered Cord
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver or clinician explicitly states 'tethered cord' diagnosis: 'tethered spinal cord', 'cord is attached', 'spinal cord tethering', neurosurgery for tethered cord, or describes a thickened rope-like filum terminale which anchors the cord at the level of L2 or below due to failure of the spinal cord to assume its normal position at L1 during embryological development — NOT for other spinal abnormalities, back problems, neurological issues without specific tethered cord diagnosis, or general neurologic signs without clear indication of abnormal tension on the spinal cord

Name: Tone, Abnormal
Notes: includes central and peripheral etiology

Name: Hypertonia
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver or clinician explicitly describes increased muscle tone: 'muscles are too tight', 'spasticity', 'hypertonia', 'stiff muscles', 'increased tone', 'stiff arms and legs that are difficult to move', physical therapy for spasticity, or when specifically referring to increased muscle tone in the arms of the affected person — NOT for normal muscle firmness, temporary stiffness, or muscle tension without increased tone

Name: Hypotonia (includes open mouth)
Notes: An abnormally low muscle tone (the amount of tension or resistance to movement in a muscle) (HPO Term: 0001252)

Name: Drooling (includes excessive salivation, sialorrhe)
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver describes persistent excessive drooling affecting daily life: 'drools constantly', 'soaks through bibs/shirts', 'excessive saliva', 'drooling all the time' - NOT for normal infant drooling, occasional drooling, or drooling only during teething/illness.

Name: Other
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver describes persistent excessive drooling affecting daily life: 'drools constantly', 'soaks through bibs/shirts', 'excessive saliva', 'drooling all the time' - NOT for normal infant drooling, occasional drooling, or drooling only during teething/illness.

Name: Abnormal Labs
Notes: ONLY use when caregiver or clinician explicitly states laboratory results are abnormal: 'blood work came back abnormal', 'lab tests showed problems', 'abnormal lab values', follow-up needed for abnormal labs, or when there are specific abnormalities in the level or function of hemoglobin, which is the oxygen-carrying protein in red blood cells (erythrocytes) — NOT for labs ordered but results unknown, normal variations, or single borderline values without clinical significance

Name: Autoimmune
